evidence: Variant asks whether every square N>=2 can be written as a ratio of products of consecutive integers ('Can every square $N\geq2$ be written...').
Variant restricts the representation question to non-squares ('Can every non-square $N\geq2$ be written...'), making squareness the load-bearing dichotomy.
The problem asks for congruence classes a_p mod primes p in (n^eps, n] such that 'every integer in [1,n] satisfies at least one of the congruences' — a covering-by-residue-classes condition.
The congruence classes range over 'all primes $n^{\epsilon_n} < p \leq n$', so the prime moduli are central to the statement.
Asks for congruence classes a_p for all primes 2<=p<=n such that 'every integer in [1,n] satisfies at least two of the congruences' — a double-covering condition by residue classes.
The moduli are exactly 'all primes 2 <= p <= n', so prime moduli are load-bearing in the statement.
The identity sums omega(n)/2^n where the docstring defines '$\omega(n)$ counts the number of distinct prime divisors of $n$', i.e. the prime omega function.

Docstring: 'Carmichael has asked whether there is an integer $n$ for which $\phi(m) = n$ has exactly one solution' — this decl is a formal statement of Carmichael's totient function conjecture question.
The problem studies f_max(n)/f_min(n) for preimages of '$\phi$ ... Euler's totient function' (Nat.totient in the formal statement).
The problem concerns 'a sequence of primes $q_1 < q_2 < \cdots$ such that $q_{i+1} \equiv 1 \pmod{q_i}$' and the growth of q_k^{1/k}.
The statement asserts 'the density of the set of integers which are divisible by some $d \equiv 1 \pmod{m}$ ... exists' (HasDensity), i.e. the natural density of a divisibility set.
Asks whether there is a prime $p \geq i$ dividing '$\gcd\big(\binom{n}{i}, \binom{n}{j}\big)$' — the statement is about common prime divisors of binomial coefficients.
The conjecture asserts existence of 'a prime $p \ge i$' dividing the gcd of two binomial coefficients.
Sylvester and Schur: 'for $1 \le i \le n/2$ there is a prime $p > i$ dividing n.choose i', a statement about prime divisors of a binomial coefficient.
The problem asks 'Is there a covering system all of whose moduli are odd (and greater than 1)?' — the odd covering system question, stated via StrictCoveringSystem.
